en.extract:
The red-breasted meadowlark (Leistes militaris) is a passerine bird in the New World family Icteridae. It was formerly named red-breasted blackbird but is not closely related to the red-winged blackbird group.


Taxonomy
The red-breasted meadowlark was formally described in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae. He placed it with the buntings in the genus Emberiza and coined the binomial name Emberiza militaris. Linnaeus cited his own catalogue of the cabinet of curiosities belonging to the king of Sweden, Adolf Frederick, as well as the description and illustration of the "Greater Bull-Finch" by the English naturalist George Edwards's that had been published in 1747. Neither Edwards nor Linnaeus knew the origin of their specimens but in 1902 the German ornithologists Hans von Berlepsch and Ernst Hartert designated type locality as Suriname. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
The red-breasted meadowlark is a small icterid, 19 cm (7.5 in) long and weighing 40–48 g (1.4–1.7 oz).  Males are larger than females.  The male has mainly black plumage, apart from a bright red throat, belly and wing epaulets. This striking "redcoat" plumage gives rise to the specific name militaris and the Trinidadian name "soldier bird". The female has buff edged dark brown upperpart feathers, buff underparts with a reddish tinge, and pale streaks through the crown and eye. Juveniles resemble the female, but are paler and lack the reddish tint to the underparts.
This species is very closely related to the white-browed meadowlark, S. superciliaris which breeds further south, and was formerly considered to be subspecies of red-breasted meadowlark. The male white-browed is easily distinguished by his bright white supercilium, but females of the two species are almost identical.  Female red-breasted meadowlarks are longer billed, smaller, and shorter winged than their relative, with more red and less streaking on the underparts.


Distribution and habitat
The red-breasted meadowlark is resident from south-western Costa Rica, which it has recently colonised, and Trinidad, south to north-eastern Peru and central Brazil.  In 2008, it was sighted for the first time in Nicaragua.
Like other meadowlarks, it is a bird associated with open country, including moist grasslands, pasture and cultivation, preferably with the odd bush or fence post for males to use as a songpost. It has benefited from the more open habitat created by forest clearance and ranching, and is extending its range. It is uncertain whether sightings on Tobago represent a small breeding population or wanderers from Trinidad or South America.


Behaviour


Breeding
In display the male flies up to 10 m (33 ft) in the air, then parachutes down on folded wings whilst singing a wheezing song, ti-ti-pee-pee-KWAAAAAA. The call is a short tsip. The red-breasted meadowlark builds a deep grass-lined open cup nest on the ground amongst tall grasses, with several nests often close together. The normal clutch is two to four reddish brown-blotched cream eggs.


Food and feeding
This gregarious bird feeds mainly on insects and some seeds, including rice, and forages on the ground like a bobolink.


References

New World Blackbirds by Jaramillo and Burke, ISBN 0-7136-4333-1
ffrench, Richard (1991). A Guide to the Birds of Trinidad and Tobago (2nd ed.). Comstock Publishing. ISBN 0-8014-9792-2.
Hilty, Steven L (2003). Birds of Venezuela. London: Christopher Helm. ISBN 0-7136-6418-5.

The '''red-breasted meadowlark''' ('''''Leistes militaris''''') is a [[passerine]] [[bird]] in the [[New World]] family [[Icteridae]]. It was formerly named red-breasted blackbird but is not closely related to the [[red-winged blackbird]] group.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCRecentChanges.htm|title = A classification of the bird species of South America: Recent Changes}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The red-breasted meadowlark was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He placed it with the buntings in the genus ''[[Emberiza]]'' and coined the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Emberiza militaris''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=C. | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturæ per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis, Volume 1| volume=v.1 | edition=10th | page=178 | publisher=Holmiae:Laurentii Salvii  | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727085 }}</ref><ref name=peters>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1968 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=14 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=175 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14481376 }}</ref> Linnaeus cited his own catalogue of the [[cabinet of curiosities]] belonging to the king of Sweden, [[Adolf Frederick of Sweden|Adolf Frederick]], as well as the description and illustration of the "Greater Bull-Finch" by the English naturalist [[George Edwards (naturalist)|George Edwards's]] that had been published in 1747.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1754 | title=Museum Regis Adolphi Frederici. Hans Maj:ts Adolf Frideriks vår allernådigste konungs naturalie samling innehållande sällsynte och främmande djur, som bevaras på kongl. lust-slottet Ulriksdahl beskrefne och afritade samt på nådig befallning utgifne af Carl Linnaeus | page=120 | location=Stockholm | language=Latin, Swedish | url=http://resolver.sub.uni-goettingen.de/purl?PPN371062853 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | last=Edwards | first=George | author-link=George Edwards (naturalist) | year=1747 | title=A Natural History of Uncommon Birds | location=London | publisher=Printed for the author at the College of Physicians | volume=Part 2 | page=82, Plate 82 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/50240796  }}</ref> Neither Edwards nor Linnaeus knew the origin of their specimens but in 1902 the German ornithologists [[Hans von Berlepsch]] and [[Ernst Hartert]] designated [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as [[Suriname]].<ref name=peters/><ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Berlepsch | first1=Hans von | author1-link=Hans von Berlepsch | last2=Hartert | first2=Ernst | author2-link=Ernst Hartert | year=1902 | title=On the birds of the Orinoco region | journal=Novitates Zoologicae | volume=9 | pages=1–135 [33] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/3268499 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=December 2023 | title=Oropendolas, orioles, blackbirds | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/blackbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=22 January 2024 }}</ref>

==Description==
The red-breasted meadowlark is a small icterid, {{convert|19|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighing {{convert|40|–|48|g|oz|abbr=on}}.  Males are larger than females.  The male has mainly black [[feather|plumage]], apart from a bright red throat, belly and wing epaulets. This striking "redcoat" plumage gives rise to the specific name ''militaris'' and the Trinidadian name "soldier bird". The female has buff edged dark brown upperpart feathers, buff underparts with a reddish tinge, and pale streaks through the crown and eye. Juveniles resemble the female, but are paler and lack the reddish tint to the underparts.

This species is very closely related to the [[white-browed meadowlark]], ''S. superciliaris'' which breeds further south, and was formerly considered to be subspecies of red-breasted meadowlark. The male white-browed is easily distinguished by his bright white [[supercilium]], but females of the two species are almost identical.  Female red-breasted meadowlarks are longer billed, smaller, and shorter winged than their relative, with more red and less streaking on the underparts.

==Distribution and habitat==
The red-breasted meadowlark is resident from south-western [[Costa Rica]], which it has recently colonised, and [[Trinidad]], south to north-eastern [[Peru]] and central [[Brazil]].  In 2008, it was sighted for the first time in [[Nicaragua]].<ref>{{cite journal |journal=[[Cotinga (journal)|Cotinga]] |title=First documented record of Red-breasted Blackbird ''Sturnella militaris'' in Nicaragua |pages=119–120 |first1=Wayne J. |last1=Arendt |first2=Marvin A |last2=Tórrez |volume=31 |date=Autumn 2009}}</ref>

Like other meadowlarks, it is a bird associated with open country, including moist grasslands, pasture and cultivation, preferably with the odd bush or fence post for males to use as a songpost. It has benefited from the more open habitat created by forest clearance and ranching, and is extending its range. It is uncertain whether sightings on [[Tobago]] represent a small breeding population or wanderers from Trinidad or [[South America]].

==Behaviour==

===Breeding===
In display the male flies up to {{convert|10|m|ft|abbr=on}} in the air, then parachutes down on folded wings whilst singing a wheezing song, ''ti-ti-pee-pee-KWAAAAAA''. The call is a short ''tsip''. The red-breasted meadowlark builds a deep grass-lined open cup nest on the ground amongst tall grasses, with several nests often close together. The normal clutch is two to four reddish brown-blotched cream [[Bird egg|egg]]s.

===Food and feeding===
This gregarious bird feeds mainly on [[insect]]s and some seeds, including [[rice]], and forages on the ground like a [[bobolink]].
